terms.definition: The main body of the boat, excluding mast, sails, and rigging.
The front of the boat.
The rear of the boat.
The left side of the boat when facing forward. Also means a harbour — context decides which.
The right side of the boat when facing forward.
A lever arm attached to the rudder head used to steer the boat.
An underwater fin at the stern that steers the boat when moved side to side.
The recessed area in the stern where the helm and crew typically sit.
The hatch and ladder leading below deck from the cockpit.
The lowest interior part of the hull where water collects.
The weighted fin extending below the hull that provides stability and prevents sideways drift. On the Django it is a lifting keel that raises for shallow water.
A keel that can be raised and lowered, letting the Django reduce draught from 2.40 m to 1.10 m for shallow anchorages and drying berths.
A horn-shaped fitting used to secure a line quickly.
A mechanical drum used to haul in and hold sheets and halyards under load.
A vertical post along the deck edge that supports the lifelines.
Wire or rope running along the deck edge between stanchions to stop crew falling overboard; the whole run is the guardrail, the individual wire a lifeline.
A storage locker in the stern, often accessed through the cockpit or transom.
An opening in the deck with a cover, used for ventilation or access below.
The flat or angled panel forming the very back of the boat's stern.
A strong metal fitting bolted to the hull where shrouds and stays attach.
The horizontal surface covering the hull and forming the walking area of the boat.
The raised lip around the cockpit or hatch that prevents water from flooding in.
The upper edge of the hull side where it meets the deck.
The height of the hull side above the waterline.
The line on the hull where the water surface meets the boat's side.
A spar projecting forward from the bow; on the Django it carries the tack of the asymmetric spinnaker.
A folding canvas-and-frame cover over the companionway that shields the cockpit from spray and rain.
A hinged arm on the tiller that lets the helm steer from the side deck while sitting out.
The principal sail set on the mainmast behind the mast, attached to the boom.
A triangular headsail set on the forestay, smaller than a genoa.
A large overlapping headsail whose clew extends past the mast.
A large, lightweight, balloon-shaped downwind sail.
A cruising downwind sail tacked to the bowsprit, flown like a big genoa for reaching; the Django's is a gennaker.
A fabric sleeve pulled down over the asymmetric to douse and hoist it under control.
A section of sail that can be folded and tied down to reduce sail area in strong winds.
A stiffening strip inserted into a pocket in the leech of a sail to support its shape.
The lower aft corner of a sail where the sheet attaches.
The lower forward corner of a sail, attached to the boom or tack fitting at the mast. Note: 'tack' also means the turn through the wind and the line on a spinnaker — context decides.
The topmost corner of a triangular sail, where the halyard attaches. Not to be confused with 'the heads' (the toilet).
The forward edge of a sail, running from tack to head; also to let a sail flap by heading too close to the wind.
The trailing (aft) edge of a sail, running from clew to head.
The bottom edge of a sail, running from tack to clew.
The curved, full shape of a sail that generates aerodynamic lift; also called draft.
Any sail set forward of the mast on the forestay; includes jib, genoa, and spinnaker.
The metal ring at the clew of a sail through which the sheet is attached.
The vertical spar that supports the sails and rigging.
The horizontal spar attached to the mast at its base that holds the foot of the mainsail.
The hinged fitting connecting the boom to the mast, allowing it to swing and pivot.
A wire or rod supporting the mast from the side, running from chainplates to the masthead.
The stay running from the bow or bowsprit to the masthead, supporting the mast forward.
The stay running from the stern or upper deck to the masthead, supporting the mast aft.
A line used to hoist a sail up the mast.
A line used to control the angle and trim of a sail. Say it with a long 'ee' — «ШИИТ», not «шит» (which is a swear word).
A line from the masthead to the end of the boom that holds the boom up when the sail is lowered.
A tackle or strut that pulls the boom down to control sail shape and prevent the boom from rising.
A line running from the end of the boom forward to the bow, preventing an accidental gybe.
A pulley used to redirect a line or gain mechanical advantage.
A drum and foil system that rolls the headsail up around the forestay for quick reefing or stowing.
A line used to pull down the reef clew and tie in a reef, reducing sail area.
A track and sliding car that lets the mainsheet attachment point move athwartships, controlling boom position.
A control line that pulls the luff of the mainsail down to tension it and move the draft forward.
A line that pulls the clew of the mainsail along the boom to tension the foot of the sail.
The sheet controlling the mainsail, usually led to a traveller or centre cleat.
The sheet controlling the headsail, typically led back to winches in the cockpit.
The line holding the tack of the asymmetric spinnaker down to the end of the bowsprit; ease it to lift the sail for a gybe.
Sailing as close to the wind as possible, typically 30–45° off the true wind.

terms.ru: курс чуть полнее бейдевинда (ветер впереди траверза)
terms.definition: Sailing with the wind forward of the beam but not as close-hauled as possible, roughly 50–70° off the wind.
Sailing with the wind directly abeam (90° to the boat), typically the fastest point of sail.
Sailing with the wind coming from behind and to one side, roughly 120–160° off the wind.
Sailing directly downwind with the wind coming straight from astern (180°).

terms.ru: зависнуть носом к ветру, потерять ход
terms.definition: Stuck head-to-wind with no steerage, unable to fall off onto either tack.
The arc of wind angles (roughly 45° either side of dead upwind) where a boat cannot sail.
The direction from which the wind is blowing; the side of the boat facing the wind.
The side of the boat sheltered from the wind; the direction away from the wind.
The wind felt on board, a combination of the true wind and the wind created by the boat's movement.
The actual wind speed and direction measured from a stationary point.
A sudden brief increase in wind speed.
Helm asks the crew if everyone is ready to tack (turn the bow through the wind).
Crew confirms they are prepared for the upcoming manoeuvre.

terms.ru: Кладу руль под ветер! (начинаю поворот оверштаг)
terms.definition: Helm's execution call for a tack — the tiller goes to leeward and the bow swings through the wind; crew let the working jib sheet fly and take up the new one.
Helm warns the crew to prepare for a gybe — centre the mainsheet and move to safe positions.
Helm checks the crew is ready before executing a gybe.

terms.ru: Поворот фордевинд! (гик пойдёт на другой борт)
terms.definition: Helm's execution call for a gybe — the stern passes through the wind and the boom sweeps across; crew control the mainsheet and stand clear.
Warning shouted when the boom is about to sweep across, typically during a gybe.
terms.example: Watch your head — boom coming across!

terms.definition: Instruction to let out a sheet to reduce sail trim or depower the sail.
terms.example: Ease the jib sheet — she's over-trimmed!

terms.definition: Instruction to pull in a sheet to trim the sail tighter.
terms.example: Sheet in until the telltales fly!

terms.definition: Turn the boat away from the wind, toward a broader point of sail.
terms.example: Bear away onto a broad reach!

terms.definition: Turn the boat toward the wind, onto a closer point of sail, and trim on.
terms.example: Harden up, we're too low for the mark!

terms.definition: Right-of-way hail: you are on starboard tack and have priority — the other boat must keep clear. The single most-shouted safety word.

terms.ru: Дай воды у знака! (пропусти к марке)
terms.definition: A hail claiming room to pass a mark or obstruction; 'No water!' denies it.
Instruction to centre the tiller/wheel so the boat runs straight.
Instruction to release a mooring line or dock line.
Instruction to hang the fenders over the side before coming alongside.
Instruction to pull in the loose part of a line and hold the boat in position.
terms.example: Take the slack on the aft spring!

terms.definition: Instruction to release the bow (forward) mooring line.
terms.example: Let go forward — hold the aft spring!

terms.definition: Crew confirms all lines are secured and the boat is made fast.

terms.ru: На берег! (сойди с концом на причал)
terms.definition: Instruction for a crew member to step onto the pontoon with a line.
terms.example: Step ashore with the bow line!

terms.definition: Instruction to release a line immediately.
Hold the jib to the windward side to push the bow around when stuck in irons or to heave to.
terms.example: Back the jib to bring her head round!

terms.definition: Called when the boat is stuck head-to-wind with no steerage.
terms.example: We're in irons — back the jib!

terms.definition: Set up the hove-to position: back the jib to windward and lash the tiller to leeward so the boat makes slow, controlled drift.
terms.example: Heave to so we can reef!

terms.definition: Instruction to push the boat away from a dock, boat, or obstacle using hands or a boathook.
terms.example: Fend off the pontoon on the bow!

terms.definition: Instruction to let out the mainsheet to reduce heel or depower the mainsail.
terms.example: Ease the main in the gust!

terms.definition: Warning to crew to grab a handhold before a wave or manoeuvre.
Instruction to secure a line to a cleat or bollard.
Instruction to take a wrap around a cleat or winch to hold a line under load.
terms.example: Take a turn on the winch before it loads up!

terms.definition: Instruction to roll the headsail away on the furler, easing the sheet while hauling the furling line.
terms.example: Furl the genoa, it's gusting hard!

terms.definition: Instruction to let go of the mooring buoy and get under way. 'On the buoy' means pick it up and make fast.
terms.example: Slip the mooring — we're clear ahead!

terms.definition: Instruction to raise the lifting keel for shoal water; 'Drop the keel' lowers it again for stability.
Urgent safety call to get a hand clear of a loaded winch before it traps fingers.
First MOB action — take the way off the boat immediately by rounding up or heaving to.
MOB action — one crew member does nothing but point continuously at the person in the water so they are never lost.
terms.example: You — keep pointing at them, don't look away!

terms.ru: Нажми кнопку «человек за бортом» на плоттере!
terms.definition: MOB action — press the MOB button on the chartplotter/GPS to mark the exact position.
terms.example: Hit the MOB button on the plotter now!

terms.definition: Crew calls out that they are tailing the line off the winch during sheeting.
Instruction for crew to winch in a sheet as fast as possible.
Instruction to immediately release a line from the winch or clutch.
terms.example: Release the old sheet — she's coming round!

terms.definition: Pulling the free end of a line coming off a self-tailing winch to help it feed and grip.
Turning the winch handle to haul in a line under load.
A winch with a high-speed and a low-speed (high power) gear, switched by changing handle direction.
A jammed override where one turn traps another on the winch drum, locking the line under load.
To let a line out slowly and in a controlled way from a winch or cleat.
To secure a line on a cleat using a figure-eight pattern with a locking hitch.
To secure a loaded line using a rope clutch or cam cleat so the winch can be freed.
A winch with a built-in jaws mechanism on top that grips the line automatically, freeing a hand.
To remove loose line before loading a sheet or halyard on a winch.
A designated space at a dock or marina where a boat is moored.
A floating platform used as a dock or walkway in a marina.
A harbour facility with berths, utilities, and services for recreational boats.
The standard knot used to secure a line to a cleat: one round turn then figure-eights with a locking hitch.
A reliable fixed loop knot that does not slip under load and is easy to undo.
A mooring line running from the stern to a dock cleat or bollard, holding the aft end of the boat.
A mooring line running diagonally along the boat to prevent it moving forward or aft along the dock.
A short mooring line running perpendicular from the boat to the dock, holding the boat close alongside.
An inflated cushion hung over the boat's side to protect it from a dock or other boat.
A general term for a mooring or towing line.
A berth alongside a short finger pontoon projecting from the main dock, making it easy to step on and off.
Mains electricity supplied from the dock via a cable plugged into the boat.
A mooring line from the bow to the dock, holding the forward end of the boat. Two words — not the 'bowline' knot.
A spring line leading from a point forward on the boat aft to the dock, stopping the boat surging forward.
A spring line leading from a point aft on the boat forward to the dock, stopping the boat surging aft.
To moor with the boat's side parallel and adjacent to the dock or another vessel.
A floating buoy anchored to the seabed that a boat picks up and makes fast to instead of anchoring.
A light line led from the pontoon to a heavy ground chain, hauled up to make the bow fast in a Mediterranean stern-to berth.
A heavy device lowered to the seabed to hold the boat in place.
The chain and/or rope connecting the anchor to the boat.
A powered or manual winch on the foredeck used to raise and lower the anchor and chain.
The ratio of anchor rode paid out to water depth; more scope gives better holding.
To pay out more anchor chain in a controlled way to increase scope.
To check a running line or the anchor chain sharply against a cleat or the windlass to stop it.
The anchor has set into the seabed and is holding; the boat 'brings up' as the rode comes taut.
A nautical map showing water depths, hazards, coastlines, and navigation marks.
An electronic device that displays GPS position on a digital nautical chart.
Global Positioning System; provides the boat's exact position using satellite signals.
A specific geographic coordinate entered into the chartplotter as a navigation target.
The compass direction from your position to a landmark or destination.
The compass direction the bow of the boat is pointing.
The intended direction of travel from one point to another.
A unit of speed equal to one nautical mile per hour. The 'k' is silent.
A unit of distance equal to 1,852 metres, corresponding to one minute of latitude.
The distance from the water surface to the seabed below the boat.
An instrument using sonar to measure water depth under the keel.
Course Over Ground — the actual direction of travel relative to the earth, accounting for leeway and current.
Speed Over Ground — actual speed relative to the earth, as measured by GPS.
An instrument measuring speed and distance through the water (not over ground).
A magnetic instrument showing direction relative to magnetic north.
The sideways drift of the boat downwind of its heading, caused by wind pushing on hull and rig.
The periodic rise and fall of sea level caused by the gravitational pull of the moon and sun.
The horizontal movement of water caused by tides, wind, or ocean circulation.
The incoming tide, when the sea level is rising and the tidal stream flows inshore.
The outgoing tide, when the sea level is falling and the tidal stream flows seaward.
The short period at high or low tide when the tidal stream is barely moving — the easiest time to manoeuvre.
The larger tidal range around new and full moon, with faster streams. Nothing to do with springtime.
The horizontal flow of water driven by the tide, given in knots and direction on charts and almanacs.
A scale from 0 (calm) to 12 (hurricane) that classifies wind speed by its effects on the sea surface.
A number on the Beaufort scale describing wind strength, e.g. Force 4 = moderate breeze.
Wind speed expressed in nautical miles per hour; 1 knot is about 0.5 m/s.
Wind with periodic short-lived increases in speed above the mean; e.g. 'gusting to 25 knots'.
A sudden, brief, intense burst of strong wind, often with rain, lasting minutes.
Long, smooth waves generated by distant storms that have travelled beyond the wind that created them.
Short, steep, irregular waves typically caused by local wind on shallow water.
How far you can see; given in forecasts as good, moderate, poor, or fog.
Thick water vapour reducing visibility below 1,000 metres; requires sound signals and a radar/AIS watch.
The compass direction from which the wind is blowing.
A clockwise shift in wind direction, e.g. from SW to W to NW.
An anti-clockwise shift in wind direction, e.g. from SW to S to SE.
In the direction from which the wind blows; also used to describe sailing close-hauled.
In the direction the wind is blowing toward; also used to describe sailing on a broad reach or run.
Wind blowing from the land out to sea, creating calm conditions near the shore but rough seas further out.
Wind blowing from the sea toward the land; can make leaving harbour difficult.
The auxiliary inboard Yanmar diesel used when there is no wind or for manoeuvring in harbour.
The lever controlling engine speed and gear (forward/neutral/reverse).
A drive leg passing straight down through the hull to the propeller, as fitted to the Django's Yanmar diesel.
The sideways kick of the stern caused by propeller rotation, strongest in reverse — use it to help spring off a berth.
Engine in forward gear driving the boat forwards. 'Slow ahead', 'half ahead', 'full ahead' set the speed.
Engine in reverse gear driving the boat backwards.
Gear disengaged so the engine runs without driving the propeller.
The engine idling at its lowest steady speed.
Engine speed in revolutions per minute; 'give her some revs' means increase power.
The boat is moving too slowly for the rudder to bite, so steering has no effect.
Running the engine with sails set at the same time, for speed or to hold course in light or foul wind.
An audible or visual warning indicating an engine fault such as overheating or low oil pressure.
The rubber pump rotor that circulates raw water to cool the engine; must be replaced regularly.
The engine stop button or pull-cable on the panel that shuts the Yanmar down by cutting its fuel supply.
Very High Frequency marine radio used for ship-to-ship and ship-to-shore communication.
The international VHF distress, safety, and calling channel that all vessels must monitor.
Automatic Identification System; broadcasts and receives vessel position, speed, and identity data.
A call (not on channel 16) asking another station to confirm how well your transmission is being received.
Standard radio phrase asking the other station to repeat their last message. Never say 'repeat' on the radio.
The international radio distress call for immediate, life-threatening danger; spoken three times on channel 16.
An urgent radio call for situations that are serious but not immediately life-threatening.
The safety-signal radio prefix for navigational or weather warnings; spoken three times, completes the Mayday / Pan-Pan / Sécurité triad.
A standardised radio call on channel 16 giving vessel name, position, nature of distress, and number of people on board.
A personal flotation device worn to keep a person afloat; ideally self-inflating with a crotch strap.
A webbing vest or jacket with attachment points for a safety tether.
A line clipped from the harness to a jackline or strong point to keep crew on the boat.
A strong line or strap running along the deck fore and aft to which tethers can be clipped.
An emergency situation where a person has fallen into the water; triggers immediate rescue procedure.
Emergency call when a person has fallen into the water — initiates the MOB procedure.
Emergency Position Indicating Radio Beacon — activates in water and transmits a distress signal to satellites.
A pyrotechnic signalling device used to indicate distress or location.
A manual or electric pump used to remove water from the bilge.
A portable device for putting out small fires on board.
An inflatable raft stowed on deck that deploys and inflates when the boat has to be abandoned.

terms.ru: буй человека за бортом (с флагом и огнём)
terms.definition: A tall floating marker with a flag and light deployed immediately when someone goes overboard to mark their position.
A bag containing a coiled floating line that can be thrown to a person in the water.
The kitchen area on a boat, with a stove, sink, and food storage.
The main living and dining cabin below deck.
A bed or sleeping place on board. Note: 'berth' also means a mooring space — context decides.
The navigator's desk below deck where charts are worked and instruments are mounted.
The toilet on a boat, and the compartment containing it. Always plural — 'the heads' — not to be confused with the head of a sail.
A valve on a hull skin-fitting that opens or closes a pipe to the sea; closing seacocks before leaving the boat is a key safety habit.
The gimballed cooker in the galley, usually gas, that stays level as the boat heels.
The pressurised cylinder supplying the stove; its seacock-style valve is shut off when not cooking, for safety.
The onboard tank holding drinking and washing water, fed to the galley and heads by a pump.
The small boat used to ferry crew between the yacht and the shore when at anchor or on a buoy.
A small open boat; a rigid-hulled inflatable is a RIB. Used as the yacht's tender.
A small petrol engine clamped to the transom of the tender.
The bow line of the tender, used to tow it or make it fast. Nothing to do with painting.
The paired blades used to row the tender when the outboard is off or fails.
To carry crew or gear from the anchored yacht to the shore in the tender.
